4-1.v: Cost behavior describes how costs change as activity levels change. Understanding cost behavior is essential for budgeting, decision-making, and performance evaluation.
Variable costs change proportionally with activity. They are CONSTANT PER UNIT but change in TOTAL.

v: Examples: Direct Materials, Sales Commissions, Packaging
Key rule: Total changes — Per unit stays the same

4-1.v: Fixed costs remain CONSTANT IN TOTAL regardless of activity. Cost per unit decreases as production increases.

v: Examples: Rent, Insurance, Salaries, Depreciation
Key rule: Total stays the same — Per unit changes

4-1.v: Mixed costs contain BOTH a fixed AND variable component. They must be separated using analytical methods.
Y = a + bX   |   Y = Total Cost, a = Fixed, b = Variable Rate, X = Activity
Example: Electricity bill = $500 base (fixed) + $0.10 per KWH (variable). At 3,000 KWH: Y = $500 + ($0.10 x 3,000) = $800.

v: Step 1: Variable Rate = (High Cost - Low Cost) / (High Activity - Low Activity)
Step 2: Fixed Cost = Total Cost at High Point - (Variable Rate x High Activity)
Step 3: Write the equation: Y = Fixed Cost + (Variable Rate x X)

4-1.v: Exam Tips: Variable cost = constant per unit. Fixed cost = constant in total. High-Low weakness = uses only 2 data points. Regression Analysis is more accurate. Sunk costs = ALWAYS irrelevant. Opportunity costs = ALWAYS relevant.

r: Cash payment involved — wages, rent, materials
Owner's time, use of own capital
Benefit LOST by not choosing the best alternative
Cost of holding inventory — storage, insurance, obsolescence
Can be started/stopped — advertising, R&D, training
Additional cost to produce ONE more unit

4-1.v: Cost Object, Cost Pool, Allocation Base & Cost Driver

r: Anything for which cost is separately measured
Group of indirect costs using the same allocation base
The cost driver used to allocate indirect costs
Factor that CAUSES costs to be incurred

4-1.v: Step costs remain FIXED within a range then JUMP when activity crosses a threshold. Step-fixed = wide steps (add a supervisor). Step-variable = narrow steps (resemble variable).
Relevant Range: The activity range where cost behavior assumptions are VALID. Outside this range, fixed costs may change and variable rates may differ.

Exam Tips: Sunk costs = ALWAYS irrelevant. Opportunity costs = ALWAYS relevant (even if not recorded). Explicit = cash paid and recorded. Implicit = no cash payment. Cost driver CAUSES costs. Allocation base IS the cost driver used to allocate indirect costs. Relevant range = where Y = a + bX holds true.

4-2.v: Why Do We Need Costing Methods?

Before diving into specific costing systems, it is essential to understand the FRAMEWORK of cost measurement and cost accumulation. Two fundamental decisions drive how a company designs its costing system.

v: Decision 1 — Cost Accumulation: How are costs collected? Job Order Costing (by job) OR Process Costing (by department/process)
Decision 2 — Cost Measurement: How are costs measured? Actual Costing, Normal Costing, OR Standard Costing

r: Most accurate / Overhead only known at year-end — slow
Best for control / Variances reveal inefficiencies

4-2.v: Product Costs vs Period Costs — Quick Review
The Cost of Quality Framework — Integration

All costing methods must consider quality costs. Whether using job order, process, or ABC costing, prevention and appraisal costs are product costs while failure costs are typically period costs (unless internal failures occur during production).

Exam Tips: Job Order = unique products. Process Costing = identical products. Operation Costing = hybrid (both). Normal Costing uses POHR — most common in practice. Standard Costing = budgeted costs used for variance analysis. Product costs = DM + DL + MOH only. Period costs = everything else. All three measurement methods end up at the same point: actual costs — they just get there differently.

r: Maximum efficiency — no waste, no breaks, no defects
Achievable under normal, efficient conditions allowing for normal waste and downtime
Used for budgeting and variance analysis — most common
May perpetuate inefficiencies from prior periods

4-2.v: Most companies use PRACTICAL STANDARDS for variance analysis because they are achievable yet challenging. Ideal standards may demotivate workers when variances are always unfavorable.
How the Three Costing Methods Converge

All three methods (Actual, Normal, Standard) end at the same place — COGS on the income statement. They differ only in TIMING and HOW overhead is assigned. At year-end, over/under-applied overhead adjustments bring all methods to the same actual cost.

Exam Tips: Job Order = unique products. Process Costing = identical products. Operation Costing = hybrid (both). Normal Costing uses POHR — most common in practice. Standard Costing = budgeted costs used for variance analysis. Product costs = DM + DL + MOH only. Period costs = everything else. Practical standards most commonly used. Ideal standards = aspirational benchmark.

4-3.v: Understanding how costs flow through a manufacturing company is fundamental to cost accounting. Costs flow through three inventory accounts before becoming an expense on the income statement.
Raw Materials → Work-in-Process (WIP) → Finished Goods → Cost of Goods Sold
The Cost of Goods Manufactured (COGM) Statement

v: Start: Beginning WIP Inventory
+ Direct Materials Used (Beginning RM + Purchases - Ending RM)
+ Direct Labor
+ Manufacturing Overhead Applied
= Total Manufacturing Costs
+ Beginning WIP
- Ending WIP
= Cost of Goods Manufactured (COGM)

4-3.v: COGS = Beginning Finished Goods + COGM - Ending Finished Goods
Raw Materials: Beginning $10,000, Purchases $40,000, Ending $8,000. DL $25,000. MOH $15,000. WIP: Beginning $5,000, Ending $7,000. FG: Beginning $12,000, Ending $9,000.

r: Costs to CONVERT RM into finished product
Full cost to manufacture a unit

4-3.v: Exam Tips: COGM flows to COGS through Finished Goods. Three inventory accounts: Raw Materials → WIP → Finished Goods. Prime Cost = DM + DL. Conversion Cost = DL + MOH (DL appears in BOTH). Period costs (selling & admin) do NOT flow through inventory — expensed immediately. Manufacturing costs are ALWAYS product costs.
Looks at total cost of ownership from the CUSTOMER'S perspective — all costs the buyer incurs during the entire time they own and use the product.

v: Purchase price
Installation & setup
Operating costs (energy, supplies)
Maintenance & repairs over product life
Disposal / decommissioning at end of life

4-3.v: Example: Machine costs $50,000. Over 10 years: energy $20,000, maintenance $15,000, disposal $5,000. Customer life-cycle cost = $90,000 — not just $50,000.

v: A cheaper purchase price may have HIGHER operating costs → higher total cost of ownership
Companies use Customer LCC as a competitive tool: lowest total cost = selling point
Customer additional costs are NOT part of the company's life-cycle cost — they are separate

4-3.v: Exam Tips: Customer LCC = buyer's perspective (purchase + operating + maintenance + disposal). Company LCC = seller's perspective (R&D + design + manufacturing + marketing + support). Neither is required by GAAP — both are internal management tools.

4-4.v: Joint products arise from a SINGLE production process that simultaneously produces TWO OR MORE products. The costs incurred before the split-off point are called JOINT COSTS and cannot be directly traced to individual products.
Split-off Point: Where joint products become separately identifiable | Joint Costs: Costs incurred BEFORE split-off | Separable Costs: Costs incurred AFTER split-off for a specific product

r: Joint Cost x (Product Units / Total Units)
Joint Cost x (Product Sales Value / Total Sales Value)
Joint Cost x (Product NRV / Total NRV)
Maintains same gross margin for all

4-4.v: Joint costs $90,000. Product A: 1,000 units at $60/unit = $60,000. Product B: 1,000 units at $40/unit = $40,000. Total = $100,000.
Should We Process Further? — Sell or Process Decision
Process further ONLY if: Incremental Revenue > Incremental Separable Costs | Joint costs are IRRELEVANT (sunk costs)
Example: Product X sells at split-off for $50. Can be processed further for $20 more to sell at $80. Incremental revenue = $30. Incremental cost = $20. Profit = $10. → Process further!

v: By-products: Minor outputs from joint production with relatively low sales value
Two accounting methods:
1. Net Realizable Value Method: By-product NRV reduces joint costs allocated to main products
2. Production Method: By-product recognized as revenue when produced

4-4.v: Exam Tips: Joint costs are SUNK at split-off — NEVER relevant for sell-or-process decisions. Only INCREMENTAL revenues and costs after split-off matter. Sales Value at Split-off is the most common allocation method. NRV method: NRV = Final Price - Separable Costs. By-products have LOW relative value. Physical units method ignores market value — may distort profitability.
Allocates joint costs so ALL products end up with the SAME gross margin percentage as the overall combined gross margin.

v: Step 1: Overall GM% = (Total Revenue - Total Costs) / Total Revenue
Step 2: Each product's Gross Profit = Revenue x GM%
Step 3: Each product's COGS = Revenue - Gross Profit
Step 4: Joint cost allocation = COGS - Separable costs

4-4.v: Example: Joint costs $100,000. Product A: revenue $120,000, separable $10,000. Product B: revenue $80,000, separable $15,000. Total costs = $125,000. GM% = $75,000/$200,000 = 37.5%. Product A COGS = $120,000 x 62.5% = $75,000. Joint cost A = $75,000 - $10,000 = $65,000.
By-Product Journal Entries — Production vs Sales Method

r: Dr. By-Product Inventory (NRV) | Cr. WIP — Main Product
Dr. Cash | Cr. By-Product Inventory + Gain

4-4.v: Exam Tips: Constant GM% = all products get same gross margin %. Production Method = by-product NRV reduces main product cost at production. Sales Method = recognized as income when sold. By-product NRV = Sales Price - Additional Processing Costs.

4-5.v: By-products are LOW-VALUE outputs that arise naturally from producing the main product. They emerge from the same joint production process but have relatively minor economic significance compared to the main product.

v: By-products have LOW relative sales value compared to main products
Emerge from the same production process — cannot be separately produced
Examples: sawdust from lumber, molasses from sugar refining, glycerin from soap production

4-5.v: NRV = Estimated Sales Price - Additional Processing Costs after Split-off
By-product NRV is recognized as soon as it is PRODUCED and DEDUCTED from the main product's cost.

r: Dr. Inventory – By-Product (NRV) 12,000 | Cr. WIP – Main Product 12,000
Dr. FG – Main Product 188,000 | Cr. WIP 188,000
Dr. Cash 15,000 | Cr. By-Product Inventory 12,000 | Cr. Revenue/Gain 3,000

4-5.v: No entry is made for by-product until it is SOLD. Joint costs remain fully assigned to the main product.

r: Dr. FG – Main Product 200,000 | Cr. WIP 200,000

4-5.v: Production Method vs Sales Method — Key Differences

r: No effect on main product cost

4-5.v: Exam Tips: By-products = LOW relative value. NRV = Sales Price - Additional Processing Costs. Production Method: Dr. By-Product Inventory / Cr. WIP (reduces main product cost). Sales Method: no entry until sold, then Other Income. Main difference: WHEN recognized.

4-6.v: Process Costing is used when companies produce LARGE QUANTITIES of IDENTICAL products continuously through a series of processes. Examples: chemicals, paint, beverages, paper, cement, and food processing.
Since some units are partially complete at period-end (Work-in-Process), we convert them to EQUIVALENT UNITS — the number of complete units that could have been produced with the same effort.
EUP = Completed Units + (Ending WIP Units x % Completion)
IMPORTANT: Materials and Conversion costs may have DIFFERENT completion %s. Materials are often added at the START (100%) while conversion (labor + overhead) accumulates gradually.

v: Weighted Average: Combines beginning WIP costs with current period costs. Simpler. EUP = units completed + ending WIP x %complete.
FIFO: Keeps beginning WIP costs separate. EUP = (Beginning WIP x % left to complete) + Units started and completed + (Ending WIP x % complete).
Step 1: Summarize the flow of physical units
Step 2: Calculate Equivalent Units of Production (EUP)
Step 3: Summarize total costs to account for
Step 4: Calculate cost per equivalent unit
Step 5: Assign costs to completed units and ending WIP

4-6.v: Completed: 3,800 units. Ending WIP: 700 units (60% conversion). Total costs: DM $42,000, Conversion $28,000. DM added at start (100%).

v: EUP (Conversion): 3,800 + (700 x 60%) = 4,220 units

4-6.v: Exam Tips: Process costing = identical products. Job costing = unique products. Weighted average is simpler. FIFO is more accurate. Materials at START = 100% complete. Conversion accumulates gradually. Normal spoilage = product cost. Abnormal spoilage = period cost (loss).

r: Allocated to GOOD units — product cost
Expensed as LOSS on income statement

4-6.v: Exam Tips: Weighted Average = simpler, mixes periods. FIFO = more accurate, separates periods. Normal spoilage = product cost (in good units). Abnormal spoilage = period cost (loss on income statement). Both Weighted Average and FIFO EUP calculations ARE testable — master both methods and the 5-step procedure.

4-7.v: What Is CVP Analysis?

Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) analysis examines the relationship between COSTS, VOLUME, and PROFIT. It is one of the most powerful tools in management accounting for break-even analysis, target profit planning, pricing decisions, and understanding business risk. CVP uses the VARIABLE COSTING framework.
Key CVP Formulas
CM = Sales Revenue − Variable Costs | CM per unit = Selling Price − Variable Cost per unit
CMR = CM / Sales Revenue = CM per unit / Selling Price per unit
BEP (units) = Fixed Costs / CM per unit
BEP ($) = Fixed Costs / CMR
Units needed = (Fixed Costs + Target Profit) / CM per unit
Sales $ needed = (Fixed Costs + Target Profit) / CMR
Worked Example
Selling price $50/unit. Variable cost $30/unit. Fixed costs $80,000. Target profit $20,000.

v: CM per unit = $50 − $30 = $20
Target profit units = ($80,000 + $20,000) / $20 = 5,000 units

4-7.v: Margin of Safety
MOS (units) = Actual Units − Break-Even Units
MOS% = (Actual Sales − BEP Sales) / Actual Sales × 100
The margin of safety shows how far sales can fall before the company begins losing money. If actual sales = 6,000 units and BEP = 4,000 units: MOS = 2,000 units. MOS% = 2,000/6,000 = 33.3%.
Operating Leverage
DOL = Contribution Margin / Net Operating Income
High DOL = high fixed costs relative to variable costs. A company with DOL of 5 will see a 5% change in profit for every 1% change in sales. High DOL = more risk but more reward when sales increase.
CVP Assumptions

v: Costs can be separated into fixed and variable components
Selling price is constant regardless of volume
Fixed costs remain constant within the relevant range
Variable cost per unit is constant
For multi-product: the sales mix stays constant
All production is sold (no inventory changes)

4-7.v: Multi-Product Break-Even
When a company sells multiple products, use the WEIGHTED AVERAGE CM based on the sales mix.
Wtd Avg CMR = (Product A CMR × Sales Mix %) + (Product B CMR × Sales Mix %) + ...
BEP ($) = Total Fixed Costs / Weighted Average CMR
Example: Product A CMR=50%, Product B CMR=30%. Sales mix: A=60%, B=40%. Wtd Avg CMR = (50%×60%) + (30%×40%) = 30% + 12% = 42%. Fixed costs $84,000. BEP = $84,000/42% = $200,000.
CVP Graph

v: The Total Revenue line starts at zero and rises with slope = selling price
The Total Cost line starts at Fixed Costs on the y-axis and rises with slope = variable cost per unit
Break-even point = intersection of TR and TC lines
Area to the left of BEP = loss zone
Area to the right of BEP = profit zone

4-7.v: Exam Tips: CM = Revenue − Variable Costs (NOT gross profit). CMR = CM/Revenue. BEP units = FC/CM per unit. BEP dollars = FC/CMR. Target profit: add target to numerator. MOS = actual − BEP. DOL = CM/NOI. High DOL = more volatile earnings. Multi-product: use weighted average CMR. CVP assumes constant selling price, constant VC/unit, constant FC total within relevant range.

4-8.v: What Is Job Order Costing?

Job Order Costing accumulates costs separately for each unique job, order, or batch. It is used when products are distinct and customizable — such as custom furniture, construction projects, printing, or professional services.
Three Components of Product Cost

v: Direct Materials (DM): Raw materials traced directly to the job
Direct Labor (DL): Labor wages directly associated with the job
Manufacturing Overhead (MOH): Indirect costs applied using a predetermined rate

4-8.v: Total Job Cost = Direct Materials + Direct Labor + Applied Overhead
Predetermined Overhead Rate (POHR)
Since actual overhead is not known during production, companies calculate a POHR at the start of the year to apply overhead to jobs.
POHR = Estimated Total Manufacturing Overhead / Estimated Total Activity Base (e.g., Direct Labor Hours)
Applied OH = POHR x Actual Activity Used by the Job
Over/Under Applied Overhead
Difference = Applied Overhead - Actual Overhead | Over-applied: Applied > Actual | Under-applied: Applied < Actual
At year-end, the difference is disposed of — typically closed to Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) for small amounts, or prorated between WIP, FG, and COGS for large amounts.
Job Cost Sheet
Each job has a Job Cost Sheet tracking all DM, DL, and OH charges. This is the subsidiary ledger for Work-in-Process (WIP) inventory.
Worked Example

Exam Tips: Job costing = UNIQUE products. Process costing = IDENTICAL products. POHR is set at YEAR START using estimates. Over-applied = CREDIT balance in MOH account. Under-applied = DEBIT balance. Closed to COGS at year-end.
Operation Costing

Operation Costing is a HYBRID system combining elements of BOTH Job Order and Process Costing. It is used when products are produced in batches where all units in a batch share the same conversion process, but direct materials differ between batches (or product types).

r: Batches — similar but with material differences
Averaged across units in the batch — like process costing

4-8.v: How Operation Costing Works

v: Work Orders: Each batch has a work order (similar to a job cost sheet) that tracks the batch
Direct Materials: Charged specifically to each work order based on the materials used for that batch
Conversion Costs: Applied uniformly to all units in the batch using a department rate (like process costing)
Result: Batches with expensive materials have higher unit costs; conversion costs per unit are the same for all batches passing through the same operation

4-8.v: Unit Cost = (Direct Materials for the Work Order / Units in Batch) + (Conversion Cost Rate × Equivalent Units)

Example: A clothing manufacturer produces Work Order A (wool coats) and Work Order B (cotton shirts) through the same Cutting and Sewing departments. Both batches use the same labor and machines — so conversion costs per unit are identical. But wool costs $30/unit and cotton costs $8/unit — so DM is traced separately to each work order.

Exam Tips: Operation costing = HYBRID of job and process. DM traced per WORK ORDER (like job costing). Conversion costs averaged per unit (like process costing). Used for BATCH manufacturing with different materials but same conversion process. Industries: apparel, food, electronics. Key phrase to identify: 'produced in batches with different materials but same operations.'
Life-Cycle Costing
Life-cycle costing accumulates ALL costs associated with a product during its ENTIRE LIFE — from design through disposal. Used for pricing decisions and profitability analysis.

v: Upstream Costs (before production): R&D, design, prototyping, testing
Manufacturing Costs: DM, DL, MOH, purchasing
Downstream Costs (after production): Marketing, distribution, customer service, warranties, disposal

4-8.v: Key difference: Life-cycle costing treats pre-production and after-sale costs as PRODUCT COSTS. Traditional costing treats them as period costs expensed when incurred.
Customer Life-Cycle Costing
Looks at total cost of ownership from the CUSTOMER's perspective: purchase price + installation + operating costs + maintenance + disposal.
Example: Machine costs $50,000. Energy $20,000, maintenance $15,000, disposal $5,000 over 10 years. Customer life-cycle cost = $90,000 — not just $50,000.
Target Costing
Target Cost = Target Selling Price - Desired Profit Margin
Market-driven approach: start with what customers will pay, subtract required profit, result = maximum allowable cost. Engineers design the product to hit this cost target.

v: 80-90% of product costs are LOCKED IN during the design phase
Value Engineering: systematically redesign to achieve target cost without sacrificing quality
Traditional costing = cost-plus pricing (cost + markup). Target costing = price-minus approach

4-8.v: Exam Tips: Life-cycle costing includes ALL phases including pre-production and disposal. 80-90% of costs locked in at DESIGN — cheapest time to reduce costs. Customer LCC = buyer's total ownership cost. Target Cost = Price - Profit. Value engineering bridges gap between estimated and target cost.

4-9.v: Manufacturing Overhead (MOH) includes ALL manufacturing costs that cannot be directly traced to specific products. Unlike Direct Materials and Direct Labor, overhead must be ALLOCATED using a systematic method.

v: Examples: Factory rent, utilities, depreciation, indirect labor, insurance, maintenance
Cannot be traced directly to a product — must be allocated
Includes BOTH fixed and variable components

r: Products pass through different departments with different overhead levels

v: Direct Labor Hours (DLH): Most traditional — works when labor-intensive
Direct Labor Cost: % of DL cost
Machine Hours: Best for automated/capital-intensive operations
Units Produced: Simple but only valid for single-product companies

4-9.v: Dept A (Labor-intensive): Overhead $200,000, DLH 40,000. Rate = $5/DLH. Dept B (Automated): Overhead $300,000, MH 60,000. Rate = $5/MH. Job uses 10 DLH in Dept A and 5 MH in Dept B. Applied OH = (10 x $5) + (5 x $5) = $50 + $25 = $75.
Service departments (HR, IT, Maintenance) support production departments but don't directly make products. Their costs must be allocated to production departments before calculating product costs.

r: Allocates service costs directly to production departments only
Allocates in sequence; once allocated, a dept receives no more
Recognizes ALL inter-service relationships using simultaneous equations

v: Small amount: Close entire balance to Cost of Goods Sold
Large amount: Prorate between WIP Inventory, Finished Goods Inventory, and COGS based on their relative balances

4-9.v: Exam Tips: Direct Method is simplest — ignores reciprocal services between service depts. Reciprocal Method is most accurate. Step-down is in between. Machine hours best for automated plants. DLH best for labor-intensive. Under-applied OH = costs UNDERSTATED in products — adjust COGS upward. Over-applied OH = costs OVERSTATED — adjust COGS downward.

r: Actual VOH - (Standard Rate x Actual Hours)
(Actual Hours - Std Hours Allowed) x Standard Rate
Actual Fixed OH - Budgeted Fixed OH
Budgeted Fixed OH - Applied Fixed OH

4-9.v: Total = Actual OH - Applied OH | Favorable: Applied > Actual (over-applied) | Unfavorable: Actual > Applied (under-applied)

Exam Tips: Variable OH Efficiency Variance driven by LABOR hours (same driver as DL efficiency variance). Fixed OH Volume Variance = production more/less than capacity level in POHR. Over-applied OH = credit balance in MOH = favorable. Under-applied = debit balance = unfavorable.

4-10.v: Factory overhead includes ALL manufacturing costs that cannot be directly traced to specific units. The accounting flow involves two accounts: Manufacturing Overhead Control (actual) and Manufacturing Overhead Applied (applied).
POHR = Budgeted Manufacturing Overhead / Budgeted Activity Level

v: Dr. Manufacturing Overhead Control (actual OH incurred)
Cr. Cash / Accounts Payable / Accumulated Depreciation / etc.
Example: Actual OH $51,000 → Dr. MOH Control 51,000 | Cr. Various 51,000
Dr. Work-in-Process (applied OH)
Cr. Manufacturing Overhead Applied
Example: POHR $20/DLH x 2,400 actual DLH = $48,000 applied → Dr. WIP 48,000 | Cr. MOH Applied 48,000

4-10.v: Under-applied: Actual OH > Applied OH → Debit balance in MOH | Over-applied: Applied OH > Actual OH → Credit balance

r: Dr/Cr COGS for full variance amount
Allocate based on ending balances of each account

4-10.v: Proration Example: Underapplied $3,000. WIP $20,000 (10%), FG $30,000 (15%), COGS $150,000 (75%). WIP gets $300, FG gets $450, COGS gets $2,250.

r: Actual VOH - (Std Rate x Actual Hrs)
(Actual Hrs - Std Hrs Allowed) x Std Rate
Actual Fixed OH - Budgeted Fixed OH
Budgeted Fixed OH - Applied Fixed OH

4-10.v: Exam Tips: MOH Control = actual (debit). MOH Applied = applied (credit). Under-applied = debit balance. Over-applied = credit balance. Immaterial → close to COGS. Material → prorate to WIP/FG/COGS. Fixed OH Volume Variance = production more/less than budgeted capacity.

4-11.v: Why ABC? The Problem with Traditional Costing

Traditional costing uses a SINGLE overhead rate based on volume (e.g., direct labor hours). This works when products are simple. But in modern manufacturing with diverse products, a single rate DISTORTS costs — high-volume products get over-costed, low-volume/complex products get under-costed.
What Is Activity-Based Costing?
ABC assigns overhead costs more accurately by identifying ACTIVITIES that consume resources and using MULTIPLE cost drivers to trace costs to products. It answers: What actually causes costs?
ABC vs Traditional Costing
4 Levels of Activity in ABC

v: Unit-level: Activities performed FOR EACH unit (e.g., machine hours, direct labor)
Batch-level: Activities performed FOR EACH BATCH (e.g., machine setups, quality inspections)
Product-level: Activities supporting a specific PRODUCT LINE (e.g., product design, engineering changes)
Facility-level: Activities supporting the ENTIRE FACILITY (e.g., plant security, building maintenance) — NOT assigned to products

4-11.v: ABC Process — Step by Step

v: Step 1: Identify the major activities (cost pools)
Step 2: Determine the cost driver for each activity
Step 3: Calculate Activity Rate = Pool Cost / Cost Driver Quantity
Step 4: Apply to each product: Activity Rate x Product Usage of Cost Driver
Step 5: Calculate total overhead per unit

4-11.v: Example

Machine setup pool $60,000 with 300 setups. Activity rate = $60,000/300 = $200/setup. Product A needs 60 setups: $200 x 60 = $12,000. Product B needs 240 setups: $200 x 240 = $48,000. Traditional costing based on DL hours would distort this significantly.
Benefits and Limitations of ABC

v: Benefits: More accurate product costs, better pricing decisions, identifies non-value-added activities, supports process improvement
Limitations: Expensive to implement, requires significant data collection, not required by GAAP for external reporting, may be too complex for simple companies

4-11.v: Exam Tips: ABC uses MULTIPLE cost pools and drivers. Traditional uses ONE rate. Unit-level varies with units. Batch-level with batches. Product-level with product line. Facility-level is NOT assigned to products. ABC corrects: high-volume = over-costed, low-volume/complex = under-costed under traditional costing.
Activity-Based Management (ABM)

ABM uses the COST INFORMATION FROM ABC to MANAGE and IMPROVE the business — not just to cost products. While ABC answers 'What do things cost?', ABM answers 'How can we reduce those costs and improve efficiency?'

r: Eliminate unnecessary inspections, reduce setup times, streamline order processing
Use ABC data for long-term decisions
Product mix optimization, customer profitability, pricing, outsourcing decisions

4-11.v: Non-Value-Added (NVA) Activities — ABM Target

v: Scheduling — waiting for production to start
Moving — transporting materials between locations
Waiting — idle time between processes
Inspecting — checking for defects after the fact
Storing — holding inventory unnecessarily
Reworking — correcting defects

4-11.v: ABM process: (1) Identify all activities using ABC, (2) Classify each as VA or NVA, (3) Eliminate or reduce NVA activities, (4) Benchmark remaining activities against best practices, (5) Continuously monitor and improve.
ABM and Customer Profitability

ABM extends ABC logic to CUSTOMERS — assigning activity costs to customers based on the resources they consume. High-maintenance customers (many orders, returns, service calls) may be unprofitable despite high revenue.
Customer Profit = Revenue from Customer − COGS − Cost of Activities to Serve Customer

Exam Tips: ABM = using ABC data for MANAGEMENT decisions. Operational ABM = reduce/eliminate NVA activities. Strategic ABM = product mix, pricing, customer selection using ABC info. NVA activities = waste (moving, waiting, storing, inspecting). ABC tells us WHERE costs occur; ABM tells us WHAT TO DO about them. Customer profitability analysis = ABM applied to customers.
The 4 ABC Activity Levels

r: Machine setups, purchase orders, inspection per batch
Plant rent, general management, building depreciation

v: Unit-level: varies with EVERY unit
Batch-level: varies with every BATCH (not every unit) — high-volume products benefit from batch economies
Product-level: supports a product line regardless of how many batches or units
Facility-level: necessary for overall operations — most difficult to assign to products

4-11.v: Stage 1: Resource Consumption Driver → allocates resources TO activities | Stage 2: Activity Consumption Driver → allocates activity costs TO products

Exam Tips: Facility-level costs CANNOT be meaningfully traced to individual products. Batch-level costs driven by number of setups/batches, NOT units. Resource Consumption Driver = resources to activities. Activity Consumption Driver = activities to products/cost objects.

4-12.v: Two methods exist for treating Fixed Manufacturing Overhead (Fixed MOH) in product costing. The difference affects inventory valuation and operating income when production differs from sales.

r: DM + DL + Variable MOH + Fixed MOH

4-12.v: Unit Cost = DM + DL + Variable MOH + (Fixed MOH / Units Produced)
Unit Cost = DM + DL + Variable MOH only (Fixed MOH is a period cost)
Absorption NI − Variable NI = Fixed MOH in Ending Inventory − Fixed MOH in Beginning Inventory
Fixed MOH $60,000. Produced 6,000 units. Sold 7,000 units. Beginning FG = 2,000 units. Ending FG = 1,000 units. Fixed MOH/unit = $60,000/6,000 = $10/unit.

v: Fixed MOH in Ending Inventory = 1,000 × $10 = $10,000
Fixed MOH in Beginning Inventory = 2,000 × $10 = $20,000
Absorption NI is $10,000 LOWER than Variable NI
(Because inventory DECREASED — more fixed MOH released from beginning inventory than deferred in ending inventory)

4-12.v: Rule of Thumb: Inventory INCREASES (prod > sales) → Absorption NI is HIGHER. Inventory DECREASES (sales > prod) → Absorption NI is LOWER. Inventory constant → NI is equal.

v: Production > Sales: Absorption NI > Variable NI (fixed MOH deferred in inventory)
Production < Sales: Absorption NI < Variable NI (fixed MOH released from inventory)
Production = Sales: Absorption NI = Variable NI (no difference)

4-12.v: Fixed MOH $60,000. Produced 10,000 units. Sold 8,000 units. Fixed MOH/unit = $6. Ending inventory = 2,000 units.

v: Absorption: Fixed MOH in ending inventory = 2,000 x $6 = $12,000 (deferred)
Variable: All $60,000 expensed immediately
Absorption NI is $12,000 HIGHER than Variable NI

4-12.v: Exam Tips: Absorption = GAAP required. Variable = management use only. When production > sales, absorption shows HIGHER income. Fixed MOH is the ONLY difference between the two methods. Contribution Margin format = variable costing income statement. Gross Profit format = absorption costing income statement.

r: GAAP required · Full cost per unit · Matches costs to revenue
Transparent profit impact · Better CVP · No production manipulation
Income manipulated by overproduction · Misleading when sales are low

4-12.v: Throughput costing treats ONLY direct materials as product costs. ALL other costs (DL, variable OH, fixed OH) are period costs expensed immediately.

r: DM + DL + Variable MOH + Fixed MOH

4-12.v: Throughput = Sales Revenue - Direct Materials | Used in Theory of Constraints (TOC)

Exam Tips: 3 methods ranked by inventory: Absorption > Variable > Throughput. Throughput = DM only as product cost. Absorption manipulation: produce MORE than you sell → defer fixed costs → higher income. Variable costing eliminates this manipulation since all fixed OH is expensed immediately.

4-13.v: What Are Shared Service Departments?

HR, IT, Legal, and Maintenance departments SUPPORT production departments but do not directly make products. Their costs must be allocated to user departments to achieve accurate product costing and fair performance evaluation.
Single-Rate vs Dual-Rate Method
Rate = Total Budgeted Cost / Budgeted Activity Base | Allocation = Rate × Actual Usage
Single-Rate Example: IT total cost $120,000. Budgeted hours 12,000. Rate = $10/hr. Dept A uses 3,000 hrs → $30,000 allocated.
Dual-Rate Method — Example
IT fixed costs $80,000. Variable rate $0.50/hr. Total budgeted hours 12,000. Dept A: budgeted 3,000 hrs, actual 3,200 hrs.

v: Fixed allocation = $80,000 × (3,000/12,000) = $20,000 (BUDGETED usage)
Variable allocation = $0.50 × 3,200 = $1,600 (ACTUAL usage)
Total Dept A = $21,600

4-13.v: KEY INSIGHT — Why Dual-Rate Is Better: Under Single-Rate, if Dept A cuts actual usage, its fixed cost allocation drops — but the company's total fixed IT costs don't change. This sends a WRONG signal. Dual-Rate fixes this: fixed costs based on budgeted (committed) capacity stay constant. Managers cannot 'game' their fixed allocation by reducing actual usage.
Three Methods for Multiple Service Departments
Direct Method
Allocates service costs ONLY to production departments, completely IGNORING services between service departments. Simplest but least accurate.

Example: IT ($60,000) serves HR 20% and Production 80%. Maintenance ($40,000) serves HR 10% and Production 90%. Under Direct Method: Ignore IT→HR and Maintenance→HR. IT to Production = $60,000 × 100% = $60,000. Maintenance to Production = $40,000 × 100% = $40,000. Total to Production = $100,000.
Step-Down Method

Allocates service departments in SEQUENCE. The first department allocated can assign costs to OTHER service departments before being closed. Once a department is closed, it receives NO MORE costs. More accurate than Direct; less than Reciprocal.

v: Order MATTERS — allocate the department that serves the MOST other departments FIRST
Once a department is closed, it cannot receive additional allocations
Captures some reciprocal services — but not all

4-13.v: Reciprocal Method

Uses SIMULTANEOUS EQUATIONS to fully recognize ALL mutual services between service departments. Most accurate but most complex. Set up equations: Total Cost of each service dept = Its own cost + % of other service dept it receives.

Example: IT cost $60,000, receives 15% of Maintenance. Maintenance cost $40,000, receives 10% of IT. Equations: IT = $60,000 + 0.15M. M = $40,000 + 0.10(IT). Solve simultaneously → allocate the solved totals to production departments.
Accuracy Ranking
Reciprocal (best) > Step-Down (moderate) > Direct (simplest)

Exam Tips: Dual-Rate: fixed by BUDGETED usage, variable by ACTUAL usage. Single-Rate: everything by actual usage. Direct Method: ignores ALL inter-service. Step-Down: partial, ORDER matters. Reciprocal: simultaneous equations, MOST accurate. Key exam point: Dual-Rate prevents managers from gaming their fixed allocation by cutting actual usage — fixed costs are committed capacity costs regardless of actual consumption.

4-14.v: What Are Standard Costs?

Standard costs are PREDETERMINED BENCHMARKS for what costs SHOULD BE under efficient operating conditions. When actual costs differ from standard costs, the difference is a VARIANCE. Variance analysis tells management WHERE costs got out of control and WHY.
Direct Materials Variances
DM Price Variance = (Actual Price − Standard Price) × Actual Quantity PURCHASED | F if AP < SP
DM Quantity Variance = (Actual Qty Used − Standard Qty Allowed) × Standard Price | F if AQ < SQ
Note: Price variance is calculated at PURCHASE (for timely reporting). Quantity variance is calculated at USE. Standard Qty Allowed = Standard qty per unit × Actual units produced.
DM Variance Example
Standard: 2 lbs per unit at $5/lb. Actual: Produced 1,000 units. Purchased 2,100 lbs at $4.80/lb. Used 2,050 lbs.

v: Standard Qty Allowed = 2 × 1,000 = 2,000 lbs
DM Quantity Variance = (2,050 − 2,000) × $5.00 = 50 × $5 = $250 Unfavorable

4-14.v: Direct Labor Variances
DL Rate Variance = (Actual Rate − Standard Rate) × Actual Hours | F if AR < SR
DL Efficiency Variance = (Actual Hours − Standard Hours Allowed) × Standard Rate | F if AH < SH
Standard Hours Allowed = Standard hours per unit × Actual units produced.
DL Variance Example
Standard: 0.5 hours per unit at $18/hr. Actual: Produced 1,000 units. Actual hours 480 hrs at $19/hr.

v: DL Rate Variance = ($19 − $18) × 480 = $1 × 480 = $480 Unfavorable
Standard Hours Allowed = 0.5 × 1,000 = 500 hrs
DL Efficiency Variance = (480 − 500) × $18 = (−20) × $18 = $360 Favorable

4-14.v: Four Overhead Variances

r: (Actual Hrs − Std Hrs Allowed) × Std Rate
Actual Fixed OH − Budgeted Fixed OH
Budgeted Fixed OH − Applied Fixed OH

4-14.v: Responsibility for Variances

r: Better supplier deal, rush orders, market price change
Waste, spoilage, poor quality materials, untrained workers
Using skilled workers for routine tasks, overtime premium
Machine breakdowns, poor scheduling, learning curve effects
Actual fixed costs differing from budget
Actual production above or below planned capacity

4-14.v: Journal Entries — Standard Cost System

v: Purchase DM: Dr. Raw Materials (std price) + Dr/Cr DM Price Variance | Cr. Accounts Payable (actual)
Use DM: Dr. WIP (std qty × std price) + Dr/Cr DM Qty Variance | Cr. Raw Materials
DL: Dr. WIP (std hrs × std rate) + Dr/Cr DL Rate + DL Eff Variances | Cr. Wages Payable
Apply OH: Dr. WIP (std hrs × std rate) | Cr. MOH Applied
Record actual OH: Dr. MOH Control | Cr. Various
Year-end: Dr/Cr OH variances | Cr/Dr COGS (or prorate)

4-14.v: Favorable vs Unfavorable

FAVORABLE variance = actual cost < standard cost = good news. UNFAVORABLE variance = actual cost > standard cost = bad news. BUT: a favorable price variance from buying cheap materials can CAUSE an unfavorable quantity variance if the cheap materials have more waste.

Exam Tips: Price/Rate variances = calculated at the ACTUAL quantity (purchased for DM, worked for DL). Qty/Efficiency variances = compared at STANDARD price/rate. Standard Qty/Hours Allowed = standard per unit × ACTUAL units produced (not budgeted). Favorable = actual < standard. Unfavorable = actual > standard. Overhead Volume Variance = only exists under ABSORPTION costing — measures production vs planned capacity.

4-15.v: Managers need to predict future costs for budgeting, pricing, and decisions. Cost estimation methods determine the fixed and variable components of a mixed cost. The three main methods are: High-Low, Scatter Diagram, and Regression Analysis.

r: Lowest — may be distorted by outliers

4-15.v: Regression analysis uses statistical techniques to fit the BEST LINE through all data points. It gives the most accurate cost equation by minimizing the sum of squared deviations.
Y = a + bX   |   a = Fixed Cost (intercept), b = Variable Cost Rate (slope), X = Activity Level

r: % of cost variation EXPLAINED by the activity driver
Probability the result is due to chance

4-15.v: Simple regression uses ONE independent variable (e.g., machine hours). Multiple regression uses TWO OR MORE variables to predict cost — more accurate when costs are driven by multiple factors.
Example: Total cost = $5,000 + $3(machine hours) + $2(labor hours). Both machine hours AND labor hours drive costs.

As workers gain experience producing a product, labor time per unit DECREASES. Each time CUMULATIVE production DOUBLES, average labor hours per unit decrease by a fixed percentage (the learning rate).
When cumulative output doubles, AVERAGE time per unit = previous average × learning rate
When cumulative output doubles, INCREMENTAL (last) unit time = previous last unit time × learning rate

KEY DIFFERENCE: Cumulative Average Model applies the learning rate to the AVERAGE of all units produced. Incremental Unit Model applies the learning rate to the LAST UNIT produced. The cumulative average model is more commonly tested on the CMA exam.

Exam Tips: Regression is MOST accurate — uses all data points. High-Low is LEAST accurate — only 2 points. R² close to 1.0 = strong relationship. R² = 0.85 means 85% of cost variation is explained by the activity. Learning curve: output doubles → average time drops by learning %. 80% learning curve is common exam example.

4-16.v: Supply Chain Management (SCM) coordinates the flow of materials, information, and money from RAW MATERIAL SUPPLIERS through MANUFACTURERS to END CUSTOMERS. The goal is to optimize the entire chain — not just individual companies.

r: All companies involved from raw materials to end customer
Suppliers and raw materials (before the company)
Distribution, retailers, end customers (after the company)
Small demand variations amplified upstream — causing excess inventory
Supplier monitors and replenishes customer's inventory automatically

4-16.v: Michael Porter's Value Chain identifies PRIMARY and SUPPORT activities that create value for customers. Cost management focuses on optimizing each activity.

v: Primary Activities (directly create value): Inbound Logistics → Operations → Outbound Logistics → Marketing & Sales → Service
Support Activities (enable primary activities): Firm Infrastructure, HR Management, Technology Development, Procurement

r: Customer WILLINGLY pays for it — changes form, fit, or function
Customer would NOT pay for it if they knew — waste
Required by business but not valued by customer (e.g., accounting, compliance)

v: Process Mapping / Flowcharts: Visualize the current process to identify waste and bottlenecks
Benchmarking: Comparing performance to best-in-class competitors or industry standards
Business Process Reengineering (BPR): Radical redesign of processes for dramatic improvement
Kaizen: Continuous small improvements by all employees
Six Sigma (DMAIC): Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control

4-16.v: Exam Tips: Value Chain = Primary + Support activities. Primary activities directly create value. Non-value-added activities = waste → eliminate. Bullwhip effect = demand distortion growing upstream. Benchmarking = comparing to best practices. BPR = radical redesign (vs Kaizen = gradual improvement). SCM goal = optimize the ENTIRE chain, not just one company. Customer defines what is value-added.

Lean management is a philosophy focused on ELIMINATING WASTE and creating value for the customer. Originating from the Toyota Production System, lean identifies seven types of waste (TIMWOOD) and works to eliminate them.

v: T — Transportation: Moving materials unnecessarily
I — Inventory: Excess stock tying up capital
M — Motion: Unnecessary worker movement
W — Waiting: Idle time between processes
O — Overproduction: Making more than needed
O — Over-processing: More work than required
D — Defects: Errors requiring rework or scrap

4-16.v: JIT is a pull-based production system where materials and products are produced or purchased ONLY when needed — in the exact quantity needed, at the exact time needed.

v: Benefits: Reduced inventory costs, less waste, faster production, higher quality, lower storage costs
Risks: Supply chain disruptions can halt production (no buffer stock), requires reliable suppliers, needs stable demand

4-16.v: Kanban is the signal system used in JIT. Cards (kanban) authorize production or movement of materials only when needed — preventing overproduction.
TOC focuses on identifying and managing the BOTTLENECK (constraint) — the process limiting overall throughput. The 5 steps: Identify → Exploit → Subordinate → Elevate → Repeat.
Throughput = Sales Revenue - Direct Materials (variable costs only)

Exam Tips: JIT = pull system (demand-driven). Traditional = push system (forecast-driven). JIT goal: zero inventory, zero defects. 7 wastes = TIMWOOD. Kanban = authorization card for JIT. TOC bottleneck = the WEAKEST LINK — focus all improvement efforts here. JIT requires reliable suppliers and stable demand. Main JIT risk = supply disruption.

v: 1. Specify Value: Define value from the CUSTOMER'S perspective — what are they willing to pay for?
2. Identify the Value Stream: Map ALL processes delivering the product — identify waste in the entire flow
3. Create Continuous Flow: Production flows SMOOTHLY without interruption or queuing
4. Establish Pull: Produce ONLY what is needed, WHEN it is needed — demand-driven (JIT)
5. Pursue Perfection: Continuously strive for zero waste, zero defects — kaizen mindset

4-16.v: SMED — Single Minute Exchange of Dies
Reduces machine SETUP/CHANGEOVER time to under 10 minutes (single-digit minutes).

v: Internal tasks: Done while machine is STOPPED — convert to external where possible
External tasks: Done while machine is RUNNING — prepare beforehand
Benefits: Faster response to demand, smaller batch sizes, less downtime, supports JIT
Lean Work Cells: U-shaped workstations. Workers cross-trained for multiple tasks. Reduces walking waste, improves communication, allows quick rebalancing

Poka-Yoke (Mistake Proofing): Devices/methods that PREVENT errors or immediately detect them. Examples: plug that only fits one way, sensors that stop machines if parts missing. Goal: eliminate possibility of human error

4-16.v: Traditional costing records costs at each stage (purchase → WIP → finished goods). Backflush costing DELAYS recording until products are COMPLETED or SOLD, then 'flushes back' to assign costs.

v: Used with JIT because inventory is near zero — no need for detailed WIP tracking
Trigger point: completion of goods OR sale of goods
Eliminates most WIP journal entries — greatly simplifies accounting
Weakness: Less detailed cost information during production

4-16.v: Exam Tips: Lean 5 Principles: Value → Value Stream → Flow → Pull → Perfection. SMED = setup time under 10 minutes. Internal = machine stopped. External = machine running. Poka-yoke = PREVENTS errors. Backflush costing = used with JIT, records costs at completion/sale not each stage. JIT is PULL; Traditional MRP is PUSH.

4-17.v: ERP systems integrate ALL business functions — finance, manufacturing, HR, sales, procurement, and inventory — into ONE unified database. This eliminates data silos and provides real-time information across the organization.
MRP is a PUSH-based production planning system that uses the Master Production Schedule (MPS) to determine WHAT materials to order, HOW MUCH to order, and WHEN to order them.

v: Master Production Schedule (MPS): What finished products to make and when
Bill of Materials (BOM): List of all components needed to make each product
Inventory Records: Current stock on hand and on order
MRP Output: Purchase orders and production orders

4-17.v: Net Requirements = Gross Requirements - On-hand Inventory - Scheduled Receipts
MRP II extends MRP to include CAPACITY PLANNING, labor scheduling, machine scheduling, and financial planning. It integrates manufacturing with the broader business.
ERP vs MRP vs MRP II

v: Benefits: Real-time data, eliminates duplicate entry, better decision-making, improved inventory management, integrated financial reporting
Risks: Very expensive to implement, long implementation time (1-3 years), requires major process changes, data migration challenges, high training costs, implementation failures are common

4-17.v: Exam Tips: MRP = materials planning only (push system). MRP II = adds capacity and labor. ERP = entire enterprise integration. MRP uses BOM + MPS + inventory records. Net Requirements = Gross Requirements - On Hand - Scheduled Receipts. ERP biggest risk = implementation failure (cost overruns, disruption). JIT = pull system; MRP = push system.

r: Maximum output under PERFECT conditions — 24/7, no breaks, no defects
Theoretical capacity minus NORMAL downtime (breaks, maintenance, holidays)
Average expected capacity over 2-3 years considering seasonal fluctuations
Used for budgeting and POHR calculation
Capacity actually used in the current period

4-18.v: Relevant costs are FUTURE costs that DIFFER between alternatives. Irrelevant costs are the same regardless of the decision and should be IGNORED.

r: Real economic cost of choosing one alternative
Differ between alternatives — affect the decision
Can be eliminated by choosing an alternative

4-18.v: Should we make a component internally or buy it from an outside supplier?
Buy if: Outside Price < Avoidable Cost to Make | Consider qualitative factors (quality, reliability, strategic importance)
Should we accept a one-time order below normal selling price?
Accept if: Special Order Price > Variable Cost per unit (AND there is excess capacity) | Fixed costs are irrelevant if capacity exists
Should we eliminate an unprofitable product, division, or segment?
Drop ONLY if: Avoidable fixed costs + lost CM > 0 (i.e., dropping improves profit) | Allocated fixed costs are IRRELEVANT
When production is limited by a scarce resource, maximize contribution margin PER UNIT OF CONSTRAINT (not per unit of product).
Rank products by: CM per unit of scarce resource = CM per unit / Resource units required per unit

Exam Tips: Relevant costs = future + differential. Sunk costs NEVER relevant. Opportunity costs ALWAYS relevant. Special order: accept if price > variable cost AND excess capacity exists. Make or Buy: compare outside price to AVOIDABLE costs only. Keep or Drop: allocated fixed costs are IRRELEVANT. Constraint decision: rank by CM per unit of constraint, not CM per unit.

4-19.v: Outsourcing Decisions

Outsourcing means having an EXTERNAL supplier perform an activity previously done internally. It is a strategic decision that goes beyond simple cost comparison — it involves competitive advantage, risk, and long-term strategy.
Financial Analysis: Make vs Outsource
Outsource if: Outside Cost < Total Avoidable Internal Cost (variable + avoidable fixed costs)
Key: Only AVOIDABLE costs are relevant. Allocated fixed costs that continue regardless are IRRELEVANT.
Qualitative Factors in Outsourcing

v: Quality control: Can supplier maintain required quality standards?
Reliability: Will deliveries be on time? Supply chain risk?
Confidentiality: Will proprietary information be protected?
Core competency: Is this activity a strategic competitive advantage? (Don't outsource core competencies!)
Flexibility: Can we quickly switch back if needed?
Workforce impact: Effect on employee morale and retention

4-19.v: Strategic Cost Management
Strategic cost management aligns COST MANAGEMENT decisions with the company's COMPETITIVE STRATEGY. There are two basic strategies:

r: Aggressive cost reduction, efficiency, economies of scale
Offer unique value customers pay premium for
Invest in quality, R&D, service — don't cut costs that support differentiation

4-19.v: Customer Profitability Analysis
Not all customers are equally profitable. Using ABC principles, customer profitability analysis assigns costs to CUSTOMERS (not products) based on the resources they consume.

v: High-maintenance customers (many orders, returns, calls) cost more to serve
Simple analysis: Contribution Margin from customer - Cost to serve that customer
Result: Identify profitable vs unprofitable customers — consider dropping or repricing for unprofitable ones

4-19.v: Balanced Scorecard & Strategy

The Balanced Scorecard (BSC) translates strategy into MEASURABLE objectives across 4 perspectives: Financial, Customer, Internal Process, and Learning & Growth. It connects daily operations to long-term strategic goals.
Balanced Scorecard — Full Coverage

The Balanced Scorecard (BSC), developed by Kaplan and Norton, is a STRATEGIC PERFORMANCE MANAGEMENT system that translates an organisation's strategy into measurable objectives across four perspectives. It balances financial and non-financial measures, and short-term and long-term objectives.

r: How do we look to shareholders?
Revenue growth, ROI, EVA, profit margin, cost reduction
Market share, customer satisfaction, retention, on-time delivery, defect rate
What must we excel at internally?
Cycle time, quality rate, process efficiency, innovation pipeline
Employee training hours, skills gap, technology capability, employee satisfaction

4-19.v: Strategy Maps and Cause-Effect Relationships

A STRATEGY MAP links the four BSC perspectives in a cause-effect chain. The logic flows UPWARD: Learning & Growth ENABLES better Internal Processes → which DELIVERS better Customer outcomes → which DRIVES Financial results.

v: Example chain: Employee training (L&G) → faster production cycle time (Process) → better on-time delivery (Customer) → higher customer retention (Customer) → increased revenue (Financial)
Every non-financial measure should have a logical path to financial results
Strategy maps make the strategy VISIBLE and testable — management can see if their assumptions hold

4-19.v: Leading vs Lagging Indicators

r: Revenue, net income, ROI, customer satisfaction score
Predictors of future results — look forward
Training hours, process improvement rate, R&D investment, employee skills gap

4-19.v: BSC uses BOTH — lagging indicators confirm past performance, leading indicators predict and drive future performance. Relying only on financial (lagging) measures misses the drivers of future success.
BSC and Performance Compensation

When BSC measures are linked to compensation, employees are motivated to improve ACROSS ALL FOUR perspectives — not just short-term financial results. This prevents the common trap of cutting costs (good for short-term finance) at the expense of customer service, quality, or employee development (bad long-term).

Exam Tips: BSC = 4 perspectives (Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth). Strategy map = cause-effect chain upward from L&G to Financial. Lagging = past results. Leading = future predictors. Both are needed. BSC prevents overemphasis on short-term financial metrics at the expense of long-term performance drivers. Kaplan & Norton developed the BSC.
Accounting Process Redesign

As organisations adopt lean and process improvement initiatives, the accounting systems themselves must be REDESIGNED to support — rather than hinder — these improvements. Traditional accounting processes often create non-value-added work that contradicts lean principles.

r: Detailed variance reports encouraging local optimisation
Simplified reporting focused on system-level performance
Standard costing encouraging overproduction to absorb fixed costs
Throughput or value-stream costing aligned with lean flow
Monthly batch reporting — information arrives too late
Real-time or weekly visual reporting on the shop floor
Complex journal entries for every inventory movement
Backflush costing — record costs only at completion
Departmental cost reports encouraging local efficiency
Value-stream P&L reports showing end-to-end performance

4-19.v: Key Principles of Accounting Process Redesign

v: Eliminate non-value-added accounting tasks: If a report is not used for decisions, eliminate it
Simplify transaction processing: Backflush costing, combined journal entries, automated data capture
Align with lean: The accounting system should SUPPORT lean behaviour — not incentivise batch production or inventory build-up
Visual management: Replace complex reports with simple visual scorecards visible on the shop floor
Faster feedback: Managers need performance data in hours/days — not at month-end

4-19.v: Exam Tips: Accounting process redesign = applying BPR/lean thinking to the ACCOUNTING FUNCTION itself. Traditional standard costing can conflict with lean by rewarding overproduction. Backflush costing supports JIT/lean. The goal: accounting should add value and support operational decisions — eliminate reports and transactions nobody uses.

Exam Tips: Outsource if outside cost < avoidable internal cost. Allocated fixed costs IRRELEVANT. Don't outsource CORE COMPETENCIES. Cost leadership = minimize costs. Differentiation = invest to maintain uniqueness. Customer profitability = CM - cost to serve. BSC = 4 perspectives (Financial, Customer, Process, Learning). Strategy drives cost management decisions.

4-20.v: What Is Process Analysis?

Process analysis examines how work flows through an organization to identify inefficiencies, waste, and opportunities for improvement. It is a core tool in lean management and business process improvement.
Process Mapping (Flowcharts)
Process maps visually document every step in a process — showing inputs, activities, decisions, and outputs. They reveal where waste occurs and where improvements can be made.

v: Current State Map: Documents the process AS IT IS TODAY — reveals all waste and inefficiencies
Future State Map: Shows the IDEAL process after improvements are implemented
Gap Analysis: Identifies what needs to change to move from current to future state

4-20.v: Value Stream Mapping (VSM)
VSM is a lean tool that maps the ENTIRE flow of materials and information from supplier to customer. It shows both value-added and non-value-added time, identifying waste in the entire value stream.

r: Time to complete ONE unit from start to finish
Total time from customer order to delivery (includes waiting)
Actual time spent working on the product (value-added)
Available production time / Customer demand rate — the production PACE needed
Process Time / Lead Time — higher is better

4-20.v: Takt Time
Takt Time = Available Production Time / Customer Demand Rate
Example: Available time = 480 minutes/day. Customer demand = 120 units/day. Takt Time = 480/120 = 4 minutes/unit. Production must complete one unit every 4 minutes to meet demand.
Throughput & Bottleneck Analysis

v: Throughput: Rate at which the system produces output
Bottleneck: The slowest process step — limits overall throughput
Improving any non-bottleneck step does NOT improve overall throughput
Only improving the bottleneck increases overall system output

4-20.v: Theory of Constraints (TOC) — Full Coverage

The Theory of Constraints (Eliyahu Goldratt) states that every system has at least ONE constraint (bottleneck) that limits its performance. Rather than optimising every process, managers should focus ALL improvement efforts on the CONSTRAINT.
The 5 Steps of TOC

r: Locate the bottleneck — the process limiting overall throughput
Get the most out of it
Maximise output from the constraint without major investment — eliminate downtime, prioritise the right products
All other processes must support the constraint — feed it at its pace, don't overproduce upstream
Invest to expand bottleneck capacity — add machinery, overtime, outsource the bottleneck operation
Once the bottleneck is resolved, a NEW constraint emerges — restart the 5-step process

4-20.v: Throughput Costing (Super-Variable Costing)

Throughput costing (also called super-variable costing) is the costing approach aligned with TOC. It treats ONLY direct materials as truly variable. All other costs — including direct labor and ALL overhead — are treated as FIXED period costs.

r: DM + DL + Fixed OH + Variable OH

4-20.v: Throughput Contribution = Sales Revenue - Direct Materials Cost
Net Profit = Throughput Contribution - Total Operating Expenses (all other costs)
TOC Product Mix Decision — Throughput Margin per Bottleneck Hour
When the bottleneck limits capacity, produce the product with the HIGHEST throughput contribution per bottleneck hour first.
Throughput per Bottleneck Hour = Throughput Contribution / Bottleneck Hours Required per Unit

Example: Product A — Sales $100, DM $40, bottleneck hours 2 → Throughput/hour = ($100-$40)/2 = $30/hr. Product B — Sales $80, DM $20, bottleneck hours 1 → Throughput/hour = ($80-$20)/1 = $60/hr. Prioritise Product B despite lower total contribution.

Exam Tips: TOC 5 Steps: Identify → Exploit → Subordinate → Elevate → Repeat. Throughput costing = ONLY direct materials are product cost. All other costs (even DL) are PERIOD costs. Throughput Contribution = Revenue - DM only. Rank products by throughput per BOTTLENECK HOUR (not total contribution margin). TOC philosophy: the whole system is only as fast as its slowest step.

Process Analysis Tips: Takt Time = Available Time / Demand Rate — sets the production pace. Cycle Time = time per unit. Lead Time includes ALL time (including waiting). Value-Added Ratio = Process Time / Lead Time. VSM shows material AND information flows. Bottleneck limits throughput — focus improvement there first. Current state map shows waste; future state map shows the ideal.

4-21.v: What Are Quality Costs?
Quality costs are costs associated with PREVENTING, DETECTING, and CORRECTING poor quality. The CMA exam extensively tests the four categories of quality costs and the trade-offs between them.
Four Categories of Quality Costs

r: Quality training, process design, supplier evaluation, quality circles
Inspection, testing, auditing, statistical quality control
Defects found BEFORE delivery to customer
Defects found AFTER delivery to customer
Warranty, returns, recalls, lost customers, legal costs

4-21.v: The Quality Cost Trade-off
Investing MORE in Prevention and Appraisal REDUCES Failure costs. The goal is to find the optimal total quality cost level.

v: Prevention + Appraisal = Costs of CONFORMANCE (doing it right)
Internal Failure + External Failure = Costs of NON-CONFORMANCE (fixing mistakes)
External failure is the MOST EXPENSIVE category — defects reach the customer
Prevention is the CHEAPEST long-term strategy

4-21.v: Total Quality Management (TQM)

v: TQM philosophy: Build quality IN from the start — everyone is responsible
Zero defects goal: Continuous improvement toward perfection
Kaizen: Japanese philosophy of continuous small improvements
Six Sigma: Statistical approach targeting 3.4 defects per million opportunities

4-21.v: Quality Cost Report

Companies prepare quality cost reports showing the % of each category to total sales. A well-managed quality program has HIGH prevention/appraisal costs and LOW failure costs. Total quality costs should decrease over time as quality improves.

Exam Tips: 4 categories — Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure. Prevention + Appraisal = Conformance costs. Internal + External Failure = Non-conformance costs. External failure is MOST EXPENSIVE. Prevention is CHEAPEST long-term. TQM = everyone responsible, zero defects goal. Kaizen = continuous improvement. More prevention spending → less failure spending.
TQM Tools & Techniques

v: 1. Quality Circles: Small employee groups meeting regularly to identify and solve quality problems — empowers workers
2. Statistical Quality Control (SQC): Control charts to monitor processes. In-control = random variation within limits. Out-of-control = assignable cause exists
3. Histograms: Bar charts showing frequency distribution of quality measurements
4. Pareto Diagram (80/20 Rule): Ranks defects from most to least frequent. 80% of problems come from 20% of causes — focus on vital few
5. Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa / Fishbone): Maps root causes of quality problems. Categories: Manpower, Methods, Machines, Materials (4 Ms)

4-21.v: Kaizen Costing

Kaizen (改善) means 'continuous improvement' in Japanese. Kaizen costing applies this philosophy directly to COST MANAGEMENT — setting cost REDUCTION targets each period and continuously working to achieve them through small, incremental improvements.

r: Continuously REDUCE costs below current actuals
Set annually based on standard specifications
Set as incremental reductions from current actual costs each period
Actual vs Target Reduction — did we improve enough?
Improvement — continuously lower the cost baseline
Continuous — monthly or quarterly improvement cycles

4-21.v: How Kaizen Costing Works

v: Step 1 — Set reduction target: Management sets a cost reduction percentage (e.g., reduce unit cost by 3% per quarter)
Step 2 — Identify opportunities: Teams analyse processes to find waste elimination, efficiency gains, materials savings
Step 3 — Implement improvements: Small changes are made continuously — no big capital investment required
Step 4 — Measure results: Actual costs tracked against the reduction target each period
Step 5 — Reset baseline: Achieved cost level becomes the new baseline for the NEXT period's reduction target

4-21.v: Key distinction: Kaizen costing is applied DURING the production phase (after the product is designed), making incremental improvements to existing processes. This contrasts with Target Costing which sets cost targets BEFORE and DURING design.
Kaizen Target Cost = Current Actual Cost × (1 - Reduction Rate%)

Exam Tips: Kaizen = continuous SMALL improvements during production. Standard costing = maintain standards. Kaizen costing = continuously REDUCE the cost baseline. Target costing = cost target set at DESIGN stage. Kaizen variance = actual cost reduction vs target reduction (not actual vs standard). Kaizen is linked to lean and TQM philosophy — eliminate waste incrementally. 'Kai' = change, 'Zen' = good.
Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)
TPM is a maintenance philosophy that aims for ZERO BREAKDOWNS, ZERO DEFECTS, and ZERO ACCIDENTS by making equipment maintenance EVERYONE's responsibility — not just the maintenance department.

r: Operators perform routine maintenance on their own equipment — cleaning, lubricating, inspecting
Scheduled preventive maintenance before failures occur
Cross-functional teams eliminate specific equipment losses
Key TPM metric: OEE = Availability × Performance Rate × Quality Rate

4-21.v: OEE = Availability × Performance Rate × Quality Rate | 100% OEE = perfect production (never achieved in practice)

TPM Goal: Move from REACTIVE maintenance (fix when broken) to PROACTIVE maintenance (prevent failures before they occur). Reduces waste from machine downtime, reduced speed, and defects caused by poor equipment condition.
ISO 9000 Quality Standards

v: ISO 9000 = internationally recognized quality management standards (International Organization for Standardization)
ISO 9001 = main certification standard — specifies requirements for a QUALITY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
Certifies that PROCESSES are consistently managed — NOT that products are defect-free
Key principles: Customer focus, leadership, process approach, continual improvement
Benefits: International recognition, customer confidence, competitive advantage

4-21.v: Exam Tips: Pareto = 80% of defects from 20% of causes — focus on vital few. Ishikawa = identifies ROOT CAUSES — 4 Ms: Manpower, Methods, Machines, Materials. Control chart: points outside limits = process OUT of control. ISO 9001 = process certification (not product quality). Quality Circles = employee-driven continuous improvement.
